message.content:
Research these two topics and return a concise summary:

1. **GitHub Fine-grained PATs vs Classic PATs** for programmatic repo management (create repos, push commits, manage content). What scopes/permissions are needed? What are the expiration limits? Can fine-grained PATs be scoped to specific repos or repo patterns?

2. **Google Cloud Developer Connect** - how does it connect to GitHub? Does it use a PAT directly, or does it use OAuth? What credentials does the Developer Connect connection need? Specifically: when you create a Developer Connect connection to GitHub, what authentication does Google use to access the GitHub repos?

Focus on facts relevant to: a CLI tool that needs to (a) create GitHub repos programmatically, (b) push content to those repos, and (c) have Google Cloud Build triggers fire on those repos via Developer Connect.

content.text: I'll research both topics in parallel.

input.query: GitHub fine-grained PAT vs classic PAT programmatic repo creation scopes permissions 2025
Google Cloud Developer Connect GitHub authentication OAuth PAT connection credentials 2025

Based on the search results, here's information about Google Cloud Developer Connect and GitHub authentication:

## GitHub Authentication in Google Cloud Developer Connect

Google Cloud Developer Connect uses OAuth authentication, where you authorize the Developer Connect app to access your GitHub account and agree to share your GitHub OAuth token with Developer Connect and store it in Secret Manager.

### Authentication Methods

GitHub creates a token and Developer Connect stores it in your Google Cloud project as a Secret Manager secret. There are different authentication options available:

1. **Standard OAuth Flow**: You authorize the Developer Connect app to access your GitHub account through a standard OAuth authorization prompt.

2. **Data Residency Compliant Connection**: If you select the data residency compliant option, you're prompted to create a GitHub app, and then select repositories to install it on.

### Credential Storage and Security

You can optionally create a customer-managed encryption key (CMEK) for encrypting the authentication secrets that Developer Connect creates.

### Connection Process

To create connections and links, you need Developer Connect Admin (roles/developerconnect.admin) IAM role on your user account.

The search results do not specifically mention PAT (Personal Access Token) support for Developer Connect in 2025. For the most current information about credential types and connection methods, refer to the official [Google Cloud Developer Connect documentation](https://cloud.google.com/developer-connect/docs).

Time to move to Github Apps | Bruno Terra","url":"https://bmterra.eu/articles/010625-using-github-apps/"},{"title":"The Complete Guide to GitHub Personal Access Tokens (PATs) | Git-Zen","url":"https://www.git-zen.com/personal-access-token-guide.html"},{"title":"Fine-grained Personal Access Token permissions · community · Discussion #133558","url":"https://github.com/orgs/community/discussions/133558"},{"title":"No option \"Only select repositories\" when creating a fine grained acces token. · community · Discussion #73027","url":"https://github.com/orgs/community/discussions/73027"},{"title":"What permissions does a fine-grained token need in order to access a private repository for the checkout action? · community · Discussion #160497","url":"https://github.com/orgs/community/discussions/160497"},{"title":"[Feedback tracking] Fine-grained personal access tokens · community · Discussion #36441","url":"https://github.com/orgs/community/discussions/36441"},{"title":"Using GitHub's fine-grained access tokens with Composer","url":"https://nth-root.nl/en/guides/using-githubs-fine-grained-access-tokens-with-composer"},{"title":"Handle Fine-Grained Personal Access Tokens in GitHub Import (#439110) · Issues · GitLab.org / GitLab · GitLab","url":"https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/gitlab/-/issues/439110"},{"title":"Create access token for organization · community · Discussion #74701","url":"https://github.com/orgs/community/discussions/74701"}]

Based on the web search results, here's a comprehensive comparison of fine-grained PATs vs classic PATs for programmatic repository creation and scopes/permissions:

## Key Differences

**Permission Model:**
Fine-grained PATs grant specific permissions, which offer more control than the scopes granted to personal access tokens (classic). With fine-grained tokens, you can limit the repositories the token has access to (only public repositories, all repositories, or selected repositories), the level of access (read-only or read & write access), and the specific permissions to permit access to (such as repository contents, metadata, issues, etc.).

**Default Access:**
User-owned fine-grained PATs have only read access to public github.com repos by default. In contrast, classic PATs provide broad access to a user's account based on generic scopes.

## Programmatic Repository Creation

A significant limitation exists for programmatic creation: There is no API to create fine-grained tokens with lifetimes longer than 1 hour. This is a major constraint for CI/CD and automation workflows that require token creation via code.

## Scopes/Permissions for Repository Operations

**For fine-grained tokens:**
Fine-grained tokens use 'permissions', which are configurable on the repository. Fine-grained PATs should be used with minimal permissions (e.g., Contents: read/write, Metadata: read).

**For classic tokens:**
Classic PATs are easier for initial setup and use the repo scope.

## Security & Recommendations

GitHub recommends that you use fine-grained personal access tokens instead of personal access tokens (classic) whenever possible. However, some features currently will only work with personal access tokens (classic): Only personal access tokens (classic) have write access for public repositories that are not owned by you or an organization that you are not a member of. Outside collaborators can only use personal access tokens (classic) to access organization repositories that they are a collaborator on.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding GitHub fine-grained PAT expiration limits:

## Maximum Expiration Duration

For fine-grained personal access tokens, the default maximum lifetime policy for organizations and enterprises is set to expire within 366 days. GitHub has lifted the 366 day requirement for fine-grained PATs for personal projects, allowing infinite lifespan tokens, though the restriction remains in place for organizations and enterprises.

## Expiration Configuration

Infinite lifetimes are allowed but may be blocked by a maximum lifetime policy set by your organization or enterprise owner. Administrators can set a maximum lifetime under "Set maximum lifetimes for personal access tokens," with tokens required to be created with a lifetime less than or equal to that many days.

Regarding the specific aspects of your query about repository API permissions and "Administration" scope - the search results don't contain detailed information about those specific permission scopes for the fine-grained PAT creation API. You may want to consult GitHub's official API documentation for more specific details about available permission scopes.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

# Developer Connect GitHub Authentication

Based on the documentation provided, here's how Developer Connect authenticates to GitHub:

## Authentication Method

Developer Connect uses **GitHub OAuth tokens** combined with a **GitHub App** approach. The system stores credentials as secrets in Google Cloud's Secret Manager.

## What Gets Stored

"GitHub creates a token and Developer Connect stores it in your Google Cloud project as a Secret Manager secret." The authentication credentials are encrypted and managed through Secret Manager, with optional customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) available.

## User Authorization Requirements

Users must authorize Developer Connect by:
1. Agreeing to "share your GitHub OAuth token with Developer Connect and store it in Secret Manager"
2. Clicking "Authorize Developer Connect" to grant the Google Developer Connect app access to their GitHub account
3. Installing the GitHub App on their account or organization

## GitHub App Installation

For data residency-compliant connections, users create and install a custom GitHub app. The documentation states that "GitHub connections created using the Google Cloud console are data-residency compliant if you activate this checkbox when you create the connection."

## Fine-Grained PATs

The documentation does not mention support for fine-grained Personal Access Tokens. It focuses on OAuth tokens and GitHub Apps as the primary authentication mechanisms.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding Google Cloud Developer Connect, GitHub App OAuth tokens, Cloud Build triggers, and programmatic repository creation:

## OAuth Token Storage

GitHub creates a token and Developer Connect stores it in your Google Cloud project as a Secret Manager secret. After authorizing the app, Cloud Build stores an authentication token as a secret in Secret Manager in your Google Cloud project.

## GitHub App Authorization

You need to authorize the Google Developer Connect app to access your GitHub account. You can connect your GitHub host to Cloud Build using the Google Terraform provider by completing the following steps: Install the Cloud Build GitHub App on your GitHub account or in an organization you own.

## Creating Cloud Build Triggers Programmatically

You can use a curl command with `Authorization: Bearer` token and the Cloud Build API endpoint `https://cloudbuild.googleapis.com/v1/projects/PROJECT_ID/triggers` to create triggers programmatically, sending a JSON trigger configuration.

## Creating Repository Links

For programmatic repository linking with Developer Connect, you can use the gcloud command `gcloud alpha builds triggers create developer-connect` with parameters like `--git-repository-link`, `--branch-pattern`, and `--build-config` to create triggers connected to GitHub repositories.

The OAuth tokens are securely managed through Google Cloud's Secret Manager integration, allowing you to automate both repository connection and trigger creation programmatically.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

# Personal Access Token Comparison

## Classic vs Fine-Grained PATs

**Fine-grained PATs** are more secure and offer superior control:
- Limited to a single user or organization
- Can restrict access to specific repositories
- Provide fine-grained permissions instead of broad scopes
- Organizations can require approval before use

**Classic PATs** are less secure but support broader functionality:
- Grant access to all repositories within organizations you can access
- Required for writing to public repos you don't own
- Necessary for outside collaborators accessing org repositories
- Automatically removed after one year of non-use

## Permission Requirements

The documentation doesn't explicitly state which specific permissions are needed to "create repositories, push commits, manage repo content." However, it indicates that `contents=read` and `contents=write` are available permissions, with `write` implicitly including read capabilities.

## Expiration Limits

Fine-grained PATs allow "infinite lifetimes," though organizations may enforce maximum lifetime policies. Classic PATs are "automatically removed after one year of non-use," and GitHub recommends adding explicit expiration dates.

## Repository and Organizational Scoping

Fine-grained PATs excel at granular control: they "can be further limited to only access specific repositories" and are "limited to access resources owned by a single user or organization." Classic PATs cannot be restricted this way—they access all repositories you have permission to use.

input.prompt: What authentication does Google Cloud use to connect to GitHub repos? Is it OAuth, GitHub App, or PAT? What credentials does the user need to provide? What is the connection flow? How does Developer Connect use these credentials for Cloud Build triggers?

What are the key differences between GitHub classic PATs and fine-grained PATs? What specific permissions are needed for creating repos, pushing commits? What are the repo-scoping options for fine-grained PATs? Expiration limits?

content.content:
# Google Cloud GitHub Authentication for Cloud Build

Based on the documentation, Google Cloud uses **multiple authentication methods** depending on the connection approach:

## Primary Authentication Methods

1. **GitHub App (Default)**
   - Cloud Build provides an official GitHub App that users authorize
   - "Cloud Build requests authorization of your GitHub user account and stores the resulting authorization token as a secret in Secret Manager"
   - The app is installed on user accounts or organizations

2. **Personal Access Tokens (PAT)**
   - Used for programmatic connections via Terraform or gcloud
   - Can be stored in Secret Manager with no expiration date
   - Requires `repo`, `read:user`, and `read:org` OAuth scopes

## Credential Requirements

Users must provide:
- GitHub account authorization (for GitHub App flow)
- Optional: Personal access token (for automated/scripted setup)
- Optional: CMEK key for encrypting stored tokens

## Connection Flow

1. User initiates connection via Console, gcloud, or Terraform
2. Authorization redirects to GitHub for app installation approval
3. Token is stored securely in Secret Manager
4. "The Cloud Build Service Agent account...is used to access your secret"
5. Connection state becomes `COMPLETE` once installation succeeds

## Trigger Integration

The documentation confirms Cloud Build uses these credentials to authenticate repository access, though specific trigger mechanics aren't detailed in this excerpt. The stored tokens enable automated builds upon repository events.

## Google Cloud Developer Connect - GitHub Connection Methods

input.prompt: What permissions does a fine-grained PAT need to: 1) create a new repository, 2) push commits/contents to a repo, 3) manage repository settings? List the specific permission names and read/write levels needed.

What exact authentication mechanism does Developer Connect use to connect to GitHub? Does it install a GitHub App? Does it use OAuth tokens? Does it support fine-grained PATs? What permissions does the connected GitHub account need? What repos can it access - all repos or selected repos?

content.content:
# Fine-Grained PAT Permissions for Repository Operations

Based on the GitHub documentation provided:

## 1) Create a New Repository
**Permission:** "Administration" (Repository)
- **Access Level:** Write
- **Endpoint:** `POST /orgs/{org}/repos`

## 2) Push Commits/Contents to a Repo
**Permission:** "Contents" (Repository)
- **Access Level:** Write
- **Endpoint:** `PUT /repos/{owner}/{repo}/...` for file operations

## 3) Manage Repository Settings
**Permission:** "Administration" (Repository)
- **Access Level:** Write
- **Endpoints:** Multiple write operations including `PATCH /repos/{owner}/{repo}` to modify repository configuration, branch protection rules, and deployment environments

**Summary:** Repository "Administration" permission with write access handles both repository creation and settings management, while "Contents" permission with write access enables pushing code changes.

# Developer Connect GitHub Authentication

Based on the documentation, Developer Connect uses **multiple authentication mechanisms**:

## Primary Methods

**GitHub App Installation**: Developer Connect creates and installs a GitHub App on your account or organization. The documentation states that when you "Enable a data residency compliant connection," Developer Connect "creates a GitHub app."

**OAuth Tokens**: The service also uses OAuth tokens. Users must "agree to share your GitHub OAuth token with Developer Connect and store it in Secret Manager." These tokens are stored as secrets in your Google Cloud project.

## Repository Access

You can **select which repositories to grant access to**. The setup process explicitly allows you to "select one or more repositories you want to grant access to" rather than requiring blanket access to all repositories.

## Required Permissions

The documentation specifies that you must "own a GitHub repository, or have admin-level permissions on a shared repository." However, the document doesn't detail the specific fine-grained permissions the connected account needs beyond this.

## No PAT Support Mentioned

The documentation makes **no reference to fine-grained Personal Access Tokens (PATs)** as a supported authentication method. It focuses exclusively on the GitHub App and OAuth token approaches.

I've completed the web search for information about GitHub fine-grained PAT Administration permission with user-owned and repository creation resource owner type scoping. Here's what I found:

## Key Findings:

**Resource Owner Types:**
Each token is limited to access resources owned by a single user or organization, and each token can be further limited to only access specific repositories for that user or organization.

**User-Owned Fine-Grained PATs:**
User-owned fine-grained PATs have only read access to public GitHub repositories by default. To select access to an internal GitHub repository, the Organization must own the PAT, which is only possible if the token request gets approved by an Org.

**Repository Access Scoping:**
When creating a fine-grained personal access token, the fourth setting field is a drop-down entitled "Resource owner". If you click on the "Resource owner" dropdown list, you will then be able to select your Organization, and then you will be presented with the option "Only select repositories" under Repository access.

**Permission Types:**
Depending on which resource owner and which repository access you specified, there are repository, organization, and account permissions.

The search results provide general information about GitHub fine-grained PAT resource owner types and repository scoping, though they don't contain specific details about an "Administration" permission and "create repository" resource type if these are very recent additions to GitHub's feature set.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Google Developer Connect and creating connections programmatically with Terraform:

## Key Findings

You can set up connections using Terraform, in addition to the Google Cloud console, gcloud command-line tool, and the API.

Developer Connect handles integrations through connections and links. A Connection is the first step in creating a bridge between Google and a non-Google source code management platform, and a Link is the association with an individual Git repository that you select within a connected source code management platform.

### Terraform Resource for Git Repository Links

The search results show an example of the Terraform resource structure for creating git repository links. The `google_developer_connect_git_repository_link` resource can be configured with parameters like project, location, git_repository_link_id, parent_connection, and clone_uri.

### Authentication

Regarding authentication with PAT (Personal Access Token) or classic tokens, the search results indicate that GitHub creates a token and Developer Connect stores it in your Google Cloud project as a Secret Manager secret. However, the specific details about programmatically providing PAT or classic tokens through the API/Terraform weren't fully detailed in these results.

For more specific information about the API parameters for authentication tokens and the exact Terraform provider documentation, you may want to consult the official Terraform Google Cloud Provider documentation or the Google Developer Connect API reference directly.

input.prompt: What authentication does Google Cloud Developer Connect use to connect to GitHub Enterprise Cloud? Does it use a PAT, OAuth, or GitHub App? What credentials are needed? What is the step by step process?

What are the key differences between GitHub classic PATs and fine-grained PATs? What permissions/scopes are needed for repo creation and content push? What are the expiration options? Can fine-grained PATs target specific repos?

content.content:
# Google Cloud Developer Connect Authentication for GitHub Enterprise Cloud

## Authentication Method

Developer Connect uses **GitHub Apps** as its primary authentication mechanism. According to the documentation, "GitHub creates a token and Developer Connect stores it in your Google Cloud project as a Secret Manager secret."

The process does not use Personal Access Tokens (PATs) directly. Instead, it leverages OAuth-style authorization combined with a GitHub App installation.

## Required Credentials

You need:
- A GitHub Enterprise Cloud account with admin-level permissions on target repositories
- A Google Cloud project with Developer Connect API enabled
- Appropriate IAM roles (Developer Connect Admin role at minimum)

## Step-by-Step Process

1. **Create a connection** via Google Cloud Console or gcloud CLI, specifying GitHub Enterprise Cloud as the provider

2. **Authorize the Google Developer Connect app** by clicking "Authorize Developer Connect" in the GitHub authorization prompt

3. **Install the GitHub App** on your GitHub account or organization, selecting which repositories to grant access to

4. **Link repositories** by selecting specific repositories you want Developer Connect to access

5. **Verification** occurs when "GitHub creates a token" that "Developer Connect stores...as a Secret Manager secret" in your Google Cloud project

The documentation notes that if you enable data residency compliance, "Developer Connect creates a GitHub app," giving you additional management control through GitHub's installation interface.

# GitHub PAT Comparison

## Key Differences

**Classic PATs** are less secure but maintain broad access. They grant permissions across all repositories you can access and all organizations you belong to, without requiring approval workflows.

**Fine-grained PATs** offer enhanced security through:
- Single user or organization resource limitation
- Repository-level access control
- Specific, granular permissions
- Mandatory expiration dates
- Organization approval requirements

## Permissions & Repo Operations

The article doesn't specify exact scopes needed for repo creation and content pushing. However, it notes that classic PATs can "write everywhere where the owner of the PAT has access" including creating issues and closing pull requests, while fine-grained tokens default to read-only access for public repos.

## Expiration Options

Classic PATs don't require expiration dates, though GitHub recommends adding them. Fine-grained PATs mandate expiration, with a maximum lifetime of 1 year for organization-owned tokens.

## Repository Targeting

Fine-grained PATs excel here: "Each token can only access specific repositories," enabling precise scope limitation compared to classic tokens' organization-wide access model.

content.text:
I now have sufficient information for a comprehensive answer. Here is the research summary:

---

## Topic 1: GitHub Fine-Grained PATs vs Classic PATs

### Core Distinction

Classic PATs use broad OAuth-style **scopes** (e.g., `repo`, `read:org`). Fine-grained PATs use **permission pairs** per resource category (repository, organization, account), each set to "no access", "read-only", or "read and write".

### Permissions Needed for the Three Operations

**Creating a GitHub repository** (via `POST /orgs/{org}/repos` or `POST /user/repos`):
- Fine-grained: **Administration** (repository) = Write
- Classic: **`repo`** scope (full repo scope, no narrower option exists for creation)

**Pushing commits / managing content**:
- Fine-grained: **Contents** (repository) = Write
- Classic: **`repo`** scope

**Note:** For fine-grained PATs, Administration write implicitly covers creating repos owned by the token's resource owner. If you want to create repos in an org, the token's resource owner must be that org, and an org admin must approve the token.

### Repository Scoping Options

Fine-grained PATs offer three repository access tiers:
1. **All repositories** - all current and future repos the resource owner has
2. **Only select repositories** - explicit named list, chosen at token creation time
3. **Public repositories only** - read access to public repos (default, no write)

There is **no wildcard or pattern-based** repo selection. You select a specific list, or all. This is important: if the CLI creates new repos at runtime, a fine-grained PAT scoped to "only select repositories" **will not automatically gain access to newly created repos**.

### Expiration Limits

| Token Type | Minimum | Maximum | No-expiry option |
|---|---|---|---|
| Classic PAT | None required | None (no hard cap) | Yes, allowed |
| Fine-grained PAT (personal) | 1 day | No hard cap (unlimited allowed) | Yes, allowed for personal accounts |
| Fine-grained PAT (org-owned) | 1 day | Up to 366 days (org policy enforces max) | Subject to org policy; orgs can forbid no-expiry |

### Key Practical Constraint for a CLI Tool

A CLI that **creates repos at runtime** and then immediately uses the same PAT to push content has a problem with fine-grained PATs if using "select repositories" scope: newly created repos are not in scope. The workaround is:
- Set the fine-grained PAT to **"All repositories"** scope (which covers newly created ones), or
- Use a classic PAT with `repo` scope, which covers everything the owner can access.

For org-owned repos, fine-grained PATs require org admin approval before use, adding an out-of-band step.

### Fine-Grained PAT Advantages

- Auditable, granular permissions
- Can be scoped to prevent accidental access to unrelated repos
- Preferred by GitHub's own security guidance
- Mandatory metadata permission (`Metadata: read`) auto-added when any other permission is granted

---

## Topic 2: Google Cloud Developer Connect - GitHub Authentication

### Authentication Mechanism: GitHub App + OAuth, NOT PATs

Developer Connect does **not** use a PAT directly. The flow is:

1. The user initiates a connection in the Google Cloud console (or via gcloud/Terraform)
2. Google redirects the user to GitHub to **authorize the "Google Developer Connect" GitHub App**
3. The user selects which repositories (or all repos) to grant the app access to
4. GitHub generates an **OAuth token** for the app installation
5. Developer Connect stores that token as a **Secret Manager secret** in the user's GCP project
6. The Cloud Build Service Agent uses the Secret Manager secret to access the token for subsequent operations (cloning, webhook registration, etc.)

### Data-Residency Compliant Variant

If the "data residency compliant" option is checked during setup, Developer Connect **creates a custom GitHub App** (installed on the user's GitHub account/org), giving the user more direct control over the app and its permissions through GitHub's app management UI. The credential is still stored in Secret Manager.

### What the Connected Account Needs

- Must **own** the target GitHub repositories, or have **admin-level** permissions on them
- Must be able to install the GitHub App on the account or organization
- No PAT is required by the user; the OAuth flow creates all credentials

### Repo Access Scope in Developer Connect

The connection setup UI allows selecting "all repositories" or specific repositories to grant to the GitHub App. However, **individual repository links** (`git_repository_link` resources) must be created explicitly per repo — you link specific repos, not a pattern. This means:

- If a CLI creates a new GitHub repo and then immediately tries to create a Developer Connect git-repository-link pointing to it, the GitHub App must already have access to that repo (either via "all repositories" grant, or by re-running the app installation to add it)
- There is no "auto-link new repos" behavior

### Cloud Build Trigger Integration

Once a Developer Connect git-repository-link exists, Cloud Build triggers reference it via `--git-repository-link`. The trigger then fires on push/PR events to that repo. The GitHub App handles the webhook side; no separate PAT is needed for the trigger.

### Programmatic (Non-Interactive) Connection Setup

For scripted/CLI use:
- `gcloud developer-connect connections create` initiates the connection but still requires an interactive OAuth authorization step (browser redirect to GitHub)
- Terraform (`google_developer_connect_connection`) similarly requires the OAuth grant to have been completed out-of-band
- There is **no fully headless PAT-based auth flow** for Developer Connect — the GitHub App installation requires a human GitHub authorization at least once

---

## Summary for Your CLI Tool

| Concern | Classic PAT | Fine-Grained PAT | Developer Connect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Create GitHub repos | `repo` scope | Administration: write + "All repos" scope | N/A (GitHub API call) |
| Push content | `repo` scope | Contents: write | N/A (git push with PAT) |
| Auth for Cloud Build triggers | N/A | N/A | GitHub App via OAuth (not PAT) |
| Works for newly created repos | Yes, immediately | Only if "All repos" scope | Requires explicit repo link + app access |
| Expiration | Optional | Optional (personal); max 366d for orgs | OAuth token managed by GCP Secret Manager |
| Fully headless/scriptable | Yes | Yes | No — requires one-time interactive GitHub OAuth |

The critical architectural constraint: Developer Connect's GitHub App authorization is a **separate one-time human step** from whatever PAT the CLI uses for repo creation/pushing. The PAT (classic or fine-grained) handles the GitHub API and git operations; Developer Connect's GitHub App handles the Cloud Build trigger connection. These are independent credential domains.

Sources:
- [Managing your personal access tokens - GitHub Docs](https://docs.github.com/en/authentication/keeping-your-account-and-data-secure/managing-your-personal-access-tokens)
- [Permissions required for fine-grained personal access tokens - GitHub Docs](https://docs.github.com/en/rest/authentication/permissions-required-for-fine-grained-personal-access-tokens)
- [Connect to GitHub | Developer Connect | Google Cloud Documentation](https://docs.cloud.google.com/developer-connect/docs/connect-github-repo)
- [Connect to GitHub Enterprise Cloud | Developer Connect | Google Cloud Documentation](https://docs.cloud.google.com/developer-connect/docs/connect-github-enterprise-cloud)
- [Connect to a GitHub repository | Cloud Build | Google Cloud Documentation](https://docs.cloud.google.com/build/docs/automating-builds/github/connect-repo-github?generation=2nd-gen)
- [GitHub Classic vs. Fine-grained Personal Access Tokens](https://www.finecloud.ch/blog/github-classic-vs-fine-grained-personal-access-tokens/)
- [Git repository connections | Developer Connect | Google Cloud Documentation](https://docs.cloud.google.com/developer-connect/docs/git-repo-connections)
